ontology.foodon_gap_note: Specific species of Australian spiny lobster not identified in FoodOn; using generic spiny lobster as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJN for Lobster used as closest parent; no Australia-specific spiny lobster entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Australian spiny lobster as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of braised Australian lobster, often shared at the table, is approximately 150 grams of lobster meat with braising broth.

nutrient_source.title: Spiny lobster, mixed species, cooked, moist heat
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data for generic cooked spiny lobster (USDA FDC ID 171897) adapted as a proxy for Australian Lobster braised in superior stock. The specific preparation with pork, Yunnan ham, and pig skin is not fully accounted for in these values, which primarily represent the lobster meat itself. Sodium may be higher in the actual dish due to the braising stock.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values for Foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index set to 1 (effectively zero) as lobster is a protein-rich food with negligible carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for non-glycemic protein foods. Confirmed by University of Sydney GI Database convention for high-protein, near-zero-carbohydrate seafood.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the sodium content (approximately 340 mg per 100 g for the lobster meat alone), which may be substantially higher in the final dish due to the rich braising stock containing pork, Yunnan ham, and pig skin; portion control and reduced-sodium preparation are advised.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Australian lobster is naturally low in carbohydrates with a negligible glycemic impact, making it a suitable high-protein food choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided the accompanying braising stock does not contain added sugars or starchy thickeners.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol and preparation-related fat and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While lobster meat is low in saturated fat, its cholesterol content (~100 mg per 100 g) and the additional fat and sodium contributed by the pork-based braising stock and Yunnan ham should be considered by individuals managing cardiovascular disease; moderate portion sizes are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Australian Lobster refers to various species of spiny lobsters (also known as rock lobsters) found in Australian waters, highly prized for their firm, sweet, and succulent meat. This particular dish, commonly found in Malaysian or Zi Char cuisine in Singapore, features the lobster braised in a rich, superior stock. The stock is typically brewed for several hours using premium pork, Yunnan ham, and pig skin, imparting a deep, savory umami flavor to the lobster. The texture of the cooked lobster is firm yet tender, with a naturally sweet oceanic taste complemented by the richness of the braising broth. Nutritionally, lobster is an excellent source of lean protein (~20.5 g per 100 g), providing essential amino acids with very low carbohydrate and fat content. It is also a good source of selenium, zinc, phosphorus, and B vitamins including B12. Due to its negligible carbohydrate content, it has a very low glycemic index, making it suitable for blood sugar management. However, the sodium and fat content can vary significantly based on the braising liquid and other ingredients used in preparation, which should be considered for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. This dish is typically served as a premium main course for special occasions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is based on generic cooked spiny lobster from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171897), as specific data for Australian Lobster braised in superior stock with pork and ham is unavailable in any approved primary source. The preparation method would likely alter the final nutrient profile, especially for fat and sodium, but the core lobster values are a reasonable proxy.
notes: The input description mentions 'Boston lobster' but the food_name is 'Australian Lobster'; this record is processed as Australian spiny lobster. The nutrient profile represents the lobster meat itself and does not fully account for the rich braising stock containing pork, Yunnan ham, and pig skin, which would add to the fat, sodium, and potentially carbohydrate content of the final dish. Gluten-free status is set to false due to the complex stock ingredients which may contain gluten. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as lobster meat contains no digestible carbohydrates per USDA FoodData Central. sugar_g is naturally zero for plain cooked lobster meat per USDA FoodData Central. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as lobster is an animal product with no dietary fibre per USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked spiny lobster. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked spiny lobster. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked spiny lobster. vitamin_e_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked spiny lobster. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention.
